segments.title: Exercise bias exposed an urgent anti-aircraft problem
segments.summary: The Royal Navy recognized that peacetime exercise attacks on capital ships distorted likely wartime targets and accelerated the search for better high-angle guns.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes a mismatch between exercise behavior and the likely realities of air attack off Norway. Pilots in exercises could concentrate on capital ships, carriers, and cruisers without the risk that would shape a real attack, while often passing over destroyers and smaller cruisers. The Royal Navy had already recognized that this was a false positive rather than a reliable guide to combat. It therefore looked for the quickest way to add effective high-angle anti-aircraft fire. Four-inch high-angle mountings were the most immediate option, while 4.7-inch guns with greater elevation and the developing 4.5-inch weapon offered other paths. The passage captures the pressure to convert a recognized tactical weakness into equipment in service before events overtook the fleet.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts exercise attack choices with wartime expectations and lists the high-angle gun solutions under consideration.
